questions.question:
The memory locations 1000 and 1010 have data values 18 and 16 respectively before the following program is executed.

MOVI  R_0, 1          ; Move immediate
LOAD  R_0, 1000(R_0)  ; Load from memory
ADDI  R_0, 1000       ; Add immediate
STOREI 0(R_0), 20     ; Store immediate

Which of the statements below is TRUE after the program is executed?

A. Memory location 1000 has value 20
B. Memory location 1020 has value 20
C. Memory location 1002 has value 20
D. Memory location 1001 has value 20
questions.to_find: Which memory location contains value 20 after execution of the given program

understand.plain: The program uses indexed (base + displacement) addressing. R_0 is first set to 1, then used to load a value from memory address 1001 (= 1000 + 1). After adding 1000 to R_0, it points to address 1001 again, where 20 is then stored. We must trace each instruction carefully to find the final store destination.

keywords.explain: Loads a constant (literal) value directly into a register; no memory access required.
Effective address = base displacement constant + register value. Used by LOAD and STOREI here.
keywords.example: LOAD R_0, 1000(R_0) with R_0=1 accesses mem[1000+1] = mem[1001].

keywords.explain: Stores a literal value at the address formed by displacement + register.
keywords.example: STOREI 0(R_0), 20 with R_0=1001 stores 20 at mem[1001].

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='240'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='8' y='8' width='484' height='224' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='34' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Instruction Trace</text><rect x='20' y='46' width='200' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>MOVI R0, 1</text><text x='300' y='66' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>R0 = 1</text><rect x='20' y='84' width='200' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>LOAD R0, 1000(R0)</text><text x='300' y='104' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>EA=1001; R0=mem[1001]=1</text><rect x='20' y='122' width='200' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>ADDI R0, 1000</text><text x='300' y='142' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>R0 = 1 + 1000 = 1001</text><rect x='20' y='160' width='200' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>STOREI 0(R0), 20</text><text x='300' y='180' text-anchor='start' class='sub mut'>EA=1001; mem[1001]=20</text><text x='250' y='216' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Result: mem[1001] = 20  →  Answer D</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four-instruction trace: R0 set to 1, loads mem[1001]=1, adds 1000 to get R0=1001, stores 20 at mem[1001].

given.aim: Determine which memory location holds value 20 after the four-instruction program executes.

terms.connects: Not accessed by any instruction in this program (only address 1001 is used by LOAD).
Not accessed by this program at all.

terms.meaning: Loads from effective address 1000+1 = 1001 into R_0.
terms.example: mem[1001] = 1 (its pre-program value), so R_0 becomes 1.
terms.connects: R_0 after LOAD = 1, which becomes the base for ADDI.

terms.meaning: ADDI makes R_0 = 1001; STOREI stores 20 at address 0+1001 = 1001.
terms.example: If R_0=1 after LOAD, then ADDI gives R_0=1001, and STOREI writes to mem[1001].
terms.connects: Final store destination is exactly mem[1001].

given.plan: Trace R_0 through each instruction: MOVI sets R_0=1; LOAD fetches mem[1001] (value 1) into R_0; ADDI computes R_0=1+1000=1001; STOREI places 20 at address 0+R_0=1001.

steps.title: MOVI R_0, 1
steps.apply: Immediate mode: the constant 1 is loaded directly into R_0. No memory access. R_0 = 1.
steps.note: Immediate mode: operand is embedded in the instruction itself.

steps.title: LOAD R_0, 1000(R_0)
steps.apply: Effective address = 1000 + R_0 = 1000 + 1 = 1001. The value at mem[1001] is loaded into R_0. mem[1001] holds its initial value of 1 (not 18 or 16, which are at 1000 and 1010). So R_0 = 1.
steps.note: Indexed addressing: EA = displacement + register. Here displacement=1000, register=R_0=1.

steps.title: ADDI R_0, 1000
steps.note: R_0 now holds an address value (1001), ready to be used by STOREI.

steps.title: STOREI 0(R_0), 20
steps.apply: Store the immediate value 20 at effective address 0+R_0 = 0+1001 = 1001. So mem[1001] = 20. Memory locations 1000 and 1010 are unchanged at 18 and 16.
steps.note: Only mem[1001] is modified. Answer D is correct.

solution.result: Memory location 1001 has value 20 after execution. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Indexed (base + displacement) addressing mode: EA = displacement + register_value. Immediate mode used for MOVI and ADDI.

questions.question:
A computer has a memory hierarchy consisting of two-level cache (L1 and L2) and a main memory. If the processor needs to access data from memory, it first looks into L1 cache. If the data is not found in L1 cache, it goes to L2 cache. If it fails to get the data from L2 cache, it goes to main memory, where the data is definitely available. Hit rates and access times of various memory units are shown in the figure.

L1 cache: Hit rate = 80%, Access time = 10 ns
L2 cache: Hit rate = 80%, Access time (including L1 cache miss penalty) = 20 ns
Main Memory: Access time (including L1 and L2 cache miss penalties) = 200 ns

Then the average memory access time in nanoseconds (ns) is ________. (rounded off to two decimal places)
questions.to_find: Average memory access time (AMAT) of the two-level cache hierarchy in nanoseconds, rounded to two decimal places

understand.plain: The processor checks L1 first. On an L1 hit (80% of accesses), the total access time is 10 ns. On an L1 miss (20%), it checks L2. On an L2 hit (80% of that 20%), the total time including L1 miss penalty is 20 ns. On an L2 miss (20% of the 20%), it goes to main memory which takes 200 ns total. We compute the weighted average of all three cases to get AMAT.

keywords.explain: The fraction of memory accesses that find the required data in a given cache level.
keywords.example: L1 hit rate = 80% means 80 out of 100 accesses find data in L1.

keywords.explain: The access time already includes the time spent at all previous (faster) levels. No need to add T_L1 again when computing L2 contribution.
keywords.example: T_L2 = 20 ns is the total time seen by the processor including the 10 ns already spent in L1.

keywords.explain: AMAT = P(L1 hit)*T_L1 + P(L1 miss, L2 hit)*T_L2 + P(L1 miss, L2 miss)*T_MM, where times are inclusive.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='8' y='8' width='404' height='284' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='34' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Memory Hierarchy</text><rect x='130' y='46' width='160' height='42' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Processor</text><text x='210' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>issues memory request</text><line x1='210' y1='88' x2='210' y2='108' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='110' y='108' width='200' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L1 Cache</text><text x='210' y='144' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>HR=80%, T=10 ns</text><line x1='210' y1='152' x2='210' y2='172' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='110' y='172' width='200' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L2 Cache</text><text x='210' y='208' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>HR=80%, T=20 ns (incl.)</text><line x1='210' y1='216' x2='210' y2='236' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='90' y='236' width='240' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='254' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Main Memory</text><text x='210' y='272' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>T=200 ns (incl.)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three-level memory hierarchy: Processor to L1 (HR=80%, 10ns), L1 miss to L2 (HR=80%, 20ns inclusive), L2 miss to Main Memory (200ns inclusive).

given.aim: Compute the average memory access time (AMAT) for a two-level cache hierarchy using inclusive access times.

terms.meaning: 80% of accesses are served by L1; total time for these is 10 ns.
terms.example: Out of 100 accesses, 80 complete in 10 ns.

terms.term: L2 hit rate = 0.80 (given L1 miss), T_L2 = 20 ns (inclusive)
terms.meaning: Of the 20% that miss L1, 80% are served by L2 in 20 ns total.

terms.term: L2 miss rate = 0.20 (given L1 miss), T_MM = 200 ns (inclusive)
terms.meaning: Of the 20% that miss L1, another 20% also miss L2 and go to main memory.
T_L2 already includes T_L1 miss penalty; T_MM already includes both L1 and L2 miss penalties. Use times directly without re-adding lower-level times.
terms.example: T_L2=20ns is total time (L1 check + L2 access), not additional L2 time only.
terms.connects: Allows direct weighted sum without recursive expansion.

given.plan: Use AMAT = P(L1 hit)*T_L1 + P(L1 miss, L2 hit)*T_L2 + P(L1 miss, L2 miss)*T_MM. The times are inclusive so apply them directly.

steps.title: Compute access probabilities for each scenario
steps.apply: L1 hit rate = 80% = 0.80. L1 miss rate = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20. Of the L1 misses, L2 hit rate = 80%, so P(L1 miss AND L2 hit) = 0.20 * 0.80 = 0.16. P(both miss) = 0.20 * 0.20 = 0.04. Verification: 0.80 + 0.16 + 0.04 = 1.00.
steps.note: The L2 hit rate of 80% applies only to accesses that already missed L1, making it a conditional probability.

steps.title: Apply AMAT formula with inclusive times
steps.formula_raw: AMAT = P(L1 hit)*T_L1 + P(L1 miss, L2 hit)*T_L2 + P(L1 miss, L2 miss)*T_MM
steps.note: Since T_L2=20 ns already includes the L1 miss penalty, and T_MM=200 ns already includes both L1 and L2 miss penalties, no extra addition is needed.

questions.formula_note: AMAT for multi-level inclusive cache: AMAT = sum_i [P(reaching level i) * T_i], where T_i is the total (inclusive) access time seen by the processor when data is found at level i.
